description.summary: An animal or animals that killed roughly 100 people, mostly women and children, in the former French province of Gévaudan between 1764 and 1767. The historical case generated nationwide panic, royal hunts, and centuries of debate over its zoological identity.
description.full: The Beast of Gévaudan (La Bête du Gévaudan) is the historical name given to a man-eating animal — or more likely a series of large canids — that terrorized the former French province of Gévaudan, in the Margeride Mountains of south-central France, between 1764 and 1767. Contemporary accounts describe a tawny, wolf-like creature larger than a wolf, with a long tufted tail and dark dorsal stripe, that targeted the head and neck of solitary herders, mostly women and children. Estimates vary; a 1987 quantitative study counted 210 attacks resulting in 113 deaths and 49 injuries, with 98 of the victims partly eaten. The case escalated into a national crisis under King Louis XV, who dispatched first the Norman wolf-hunter Jean-Charles-Marc-Antoine Vaumesle d'Enneval and then his own gun-bearer François Antoine. Antoine killed a large wolf at the Chazes Abbey on 21 September 1765, but attacks resumed in December. The killings finally ended after local hunter Jean Chastel shot a creature on 19 June 1767 during a hunt organized by the Marquis d'Apcher. Modern scholars overwhelmingly favour wolves (single, multiple, or wolf-dog hybrids) as the explanation.

etymology.origin: French. Gévaudan was the historical pre-revolutionary province corresponding largely to modern Lozère and parts of Haute-Loire.
etymology.meaning: 'The Beast of Gévaudan' — the unnamed man-eating animal of that province.
etymology.first_use: Contemporary 1764–1767 French press, especially the Courrier d'Avignon and the Gazette de France

related_phenomena.name: Wolves of Paris
related_phenomena.description: 1450 wolf attacks in and around Paris

coloration: with dark dorsal stripe and tuft on the tail

features.feature: Wolf-like body, larger than a wolf
features.description: Contemporary witnesses described a creature 'like a wolf, yet not a wolf', notably larger than local wolves
A tail described as longer than a wolf's, ending in a tuft
A dark stripe down the back is recurrent in contemporary descriptions
Reports emphasize formidable teeth and bite power

physical.morphology: Large quadrupedal canid; modern reconstructions favour a very large wolf, multiple wolves acting independently or in pack, or a wolf-dog hybrid. Some 19th- and 20th-century writers proposed escaped exotic predators (hyena, lion-dog), but these explanations are not supported by primary evidence.

abilities.description: Reports note the beast's preference for lone women, children, and herders, often striking the throat or face
Survived multiple massive royal-sponsored hunts before being killed

behavior.diet: Human prey (women, children, isolated herders) and presumably livestock between attacks
patterns: Attacks mostly during daylight herding hours
Preferential targeting of head, throat, and face
Geographic dispersal across roughly 90 × 80 km (56 × 50 mi)

range.description: Former French province of Gévaudan in the Margeride Mountains of south-central France, corresponding mainly to the modern department of Lozère and parts of Haute-Loire.

hotspots.name: Mercoire Forest near Langogne

location.description: Forest near Langogne in eastern Gévaudan, site of the first reported attack

hotspots.notes: Site of the first encounter (early summer 1764) and the first official victim, Jeanne Boulet (30 June 1764)

hotspots.name: Mont Mouchet / La Sogne d'Auvers

location.description: Site near La Sogne d'Auvers where Jean Chastel shot the beast on 19 June 1767

hotspots.notes: Site of the killing of the beast by Jean Chastel during a hunt organized by the Marquis d'Apcher

first_recorded.source: Contemporary 1764 French press (Courrier d'Avignon, Gazette de France)

peak_activity_periods.description: First wave of attacks, ending with François Antoine's killing of a large wolf at Chazes Abbey (21 September 1765)
Second wave; Marquis d'Apcher organizes the final hunt; Jean Chastel kills the beast on 19 June 1767

origins.cultural_roots: Ancien régime rural France, where wolf attacks on humans were not unknown but rarely on this scale.
origins.earliest_accounts: First encounter in early summer 1764 in the Mercoire Forest near Langogne; first recorded killing of Jeanne Boulet, age 14, on 30 June 1764 near Les Hubacs.
origins.folklore_connections: Although understood at the time within ordinary categories of dangerous wolves and (in some accounts) supernatural beings, the case became a touchstone of French werewolf and bête anthropophage folklore.

timeline.event: First reported encounter: a young cowherd in the Mercoire Forest near Langogne is attacked by a beast 'like a wolf, yet not a wolf', driven off by her cattle
timeline.source: Smith, J. M. (2011), Monsters of the Gévaudan

timeline.event: Jeanne Boulet, age 14, is killed near Les Hubacs (commune of Saint-Étienne-de-Lugdarès) — first officially recorded victim
François Antoine, the king's gunbearer, kills a very large wolf at the Chazes Abbey; the corpse is sent to Versailles
timeline.significance: Royal hunt's apparent success; attacks briefly subside

timeline.event: Attacks resume in December 1765 in southern Gévaudan
timeline.significance: Demonstrates that the original beast was not the killed wolf, or that multiple animals were involved
timeline.source: Smith, J. M. (2011), Monsters of the Gévaudan

timeline.event: Jean Chastel kills a large canid during a hunt organized by the Marquis d'Apcher near La Sogne d'Auvers; attacks cease
timeline.source: Marin Report (autopsy by surgeon Antoine Boulanger / Marin)

history.modern_era: The case has been studied extensively by historians, zoologists, and folklorists. Jay M. Smith's 2011 academic monograph Monsters of the Gévaudan reads the affair as an event in 18th-century media culture as much as a wildlife crisis. Modern museums in Saugues and Marvejols, and the Maison de la Bête in Auvers, host exhibits. Modern zoological consensus favours wolves (likely multiple animals across three years), with wolf-dog hybridization sometimes proposed.

notable.name: Killing of Jeanne Boulet

location.description: Near Les Hubacs (commune of Saint-Étienne-de-Lugdarès), eastern Gévaudan

notable.description: Jeanne Boulet, a 14-year-old girl, was killed by the beast near Les Hubacs. Her death was recorded in the parish register and is the first official victim of the Beast of Gévaudan.

credibility.notes: First official victim of the Beast.

sources: Smith, J. M. (2011), Monsters of the Gévaudan

notable.name: François Antoine kills the 'wolf of Chazes'

credibility_factors: Carcass examined and shipped to Versailles

notable.description: On 21 September 1765, François Antoine and his nephew shot a large wolf in the forest of Chazes; the animal was about 1.7 m (5 ft 7 in) long. The carcass was stuffed and sent to the royal court at Versailles. Antoine was rewarded with money and honors. Attacks briefly stopped, but resumed in December.

credibility.notes: The killed wolf was not in fact the (or not the only) Beast.

sources: Smith, J. M. (2011), Monsters of the Gévaudan

notable.name: Jean Chastel kills the Beast

location.description: Hunt near La Sogne d'Auvers, on Mont Mouchet, organized by the Marquis d'Apcher

types: Other members of the Marquis d'Apcher's hunt
credibility_factors: Subsequent autopsy by surgeon Antoine Boulanger ('Marin Report')

notable.description: During a hunt organized by the Marquis d'Apcher on Mont Mouchet, local hunter Jean Chastel shot and killed a large canid. The carcass was paraded through the region and examined by surgeon Antoine Boulanger; the resulting Marin Report described an animal larger than a wolf with unusual dentition. After this killing, the attacks ceased.
sources: Smith, J. M. (2011), Monsters of the Gévaudan

physical.name: Marin Report (Antoine Boulanger autopsy)
physical.description: Contemporary autopsy of the canid killed by Jean Chastel on 19 June 1767, describing an animal larger than a wolf and detailing dentition.
physical.analysis: The autopsy is preserved in archives but the carcass itself was lost (sent to Versailles in summer heat and reportedly disposed of due to decomposition).

documentary.name: Courrier d'Avignon and Gazette de France coverage (1764–1767)
documentary.description: Contemporary French press coverage of the attacks, hunts, and royal interventions.
documentary.analysis: A primary historical source; analysed extensively in Jay M. Smith's Monsters of the Gévaudan (2011).
documentary.source: Bibliothèque nationale de France press archives

paranormal.name: Werewolf / loup-garou
paranormal.description: Contemporary rural belief that the Beast was a werewolf or supernatural creature, sometimes blamed on a particular suspect (folklore around Jean Chastel's son Antoine).

scientific.name: Multiple wolves
scientific.description: The attacks across three years and 90 × 80 km were almost certainly the work of multiple wolves rather than a single individual, possibly several distinct man-eaters in succession.
proponents: Most modern historians and zoologists, including Jay M. Smith (2011)
evidence_cited: Resumption of attacks after Antoine's 1765 wolf was killed
Wolf population in Gévaudan at the time

scientific.name: Wolf-dog hybrid
scientific.description: Some descriptions (size, coat, dorsal stripe) are consistent with a wolf-dog hybrid, possibly resulting from local mastiffs interbreeding with wolves.
evidence_cited: Behavioural boldness atypical of wild wolves

scientific.name: Exotic predator hypothesis
scientific.description: Older speculation suggested an escaped exotic animal (hyena, lion, or trained 'killer dog'). Most historians reject this for lack of evidence.

skeptical.name: Media-amplified panic over real but ordinary wolf attacks
skeptical.description: Real wolf attacks were amplified by France's first national news media into a singular 'Beast'; folklore consolidated separate incidents into one creature.
proponents: Jay M. Smith (2011), Monsters of the Gévaudan

stories: The Marquis d'Apcher's hunt and Jean Chastel's killing of the Beast
Marie-Jeanne Valet's reported single combat with the Beast (11 August 1765)
folklore.beliefs: In contemporary Gévaudan villages, the Beast was variously understood as a very large wolf, a divine punishment, or a werewolf. Today it is a centerpiece of regional identity and tourism.

literature: Jay M. Smith, Monsters of the Gévaudan: The Making of a Beast (Harvard University Press, 2011)
Henri Pourrat, Histoire fidèle de la bête en Gévaudan (1946)
Robert Louis Stevenson, Travels with a Donkey in the Cévennes (1879) — references the Beast
film_tv: Brotherhood of the Wolf / Le Pacte des loups (2001, Christophe Gans)
games: Assassin's Creed Unity: Dead Kings (2015) — 'Beast of Saint-Denis' subplot inspired by Gévaudan
other: Musée Fantastique de la Bête du Gévaudan (Saugues)
Statue of Marie-Jeanne Valet at Auvers

researchers.name: Jay M. Smith
researchers.affiliation: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill (history)
researchers.contribution: Definitive academic monograph: Monsters of the Gévaudan (2011)

organizations.name: Musée Fantastique de la Bête du Gévaudan (Saugues)
organizations.focus: Local museum dedicated to the case

research.ongoing_studies: Ongoing historical research and occasional zoological papers. Local archives in Lozère and Haute-Loire continue to be re-examined.

sources.title: Monsters of the Gévaudan: The Making of a Beast
What Was the Beast of Gévaudan?
When the Beast of Gévaudan Terrorized France
Did a 'werewolf' really terrorize France in the 1700s?
